literal: person, man, the Person; in Vedic usage often the cosmic Person (Puruṣa-Sūkta, Ṛgveda 10.90)
invariant_definition: *Puruṣa* is the conscious pole contrasted with the material-causal order. In the strict Sāṃkhya-Yoga register, this means the witness that does not act, change, or evolve, though agency and enjoyment are imputed to it through conjunction with *prakṛti*, *buddhi*, and *ahaṅkāra*. That register is foundational for later Vedānta even where it is rejected or reworked. Hence the term moves across three linked uses: the Vedic cosmic Person, the Sāṃkhya plurality of indifferent witnesses, and the Vedāntic supreme Person in expressions such as *puruṣottama*. What matters philosophically is that *puruṣa* is not merely 'person' in a modern moral or juridical sense. It names the conscious principle as distinct from the body-mind complex. The main disputes concern whether *puruṣa*s are many or one, whether witnesshood exhausts the conscious principle, and whether the supreme reality is best described impersonally as consciousness itself or personally as the highest *puruṣa*. Even schools that identify *puruṣa* with Bhagavān continue to define bondage through a confusion between the conscious principle and the *prakṛti* apparatus.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, the `hṛdyantarjyotiḥ puruṣaḥ` of śruti is finally read at the level of `lakṣyārtha`: the term does not terminate in the empirical agent but in the self disclosed when the `tvam`-padārtha is stripped of `upādhi` and reduced to pure witness-consciousness [[SB 10](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/10)]. The many conscious subjects are therefore only apparent delimitations of the one ātman, since `jīvātmanor ananyatva` alone is ultimately defensible and plurality has only a secondary pedagogical standing [[MK 3.13](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.13)] [[MK 3.14](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.14)].

Puruṣottama names Nārāyaṇa, the defect-free brahman endowed with innumerable `kalyāṇa-guṇa`s, who stands as `antaryāmin` and `sarvātman` with respect to the entire `cid-acid` complex [[ŚrīBh 6](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/6)] [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. The finite selves are real and irreducible, yet their fulfillment lies in `dhyāna`, `arcana`, and `praṇāma` directed to the feet of the `paramapuruṣa`, while the world as a whole is intelligible as ordered under one `eka-pradhāna-puruṣa` [[VS §3](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3)] [[ĪS 18](cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/18)].

The Gītā's distinction between `kṣara`, `akṣara`, and `uttamaḥ puruṣaḥ` is read as an ontological triad: perishable beings, imperishable selves, and beyond both the unique Paramātman, Viṣṇu, who alone is `puruṣottama` [[Gītā 15.16](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/15.16)] [[Gītā 15.17](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/15.17)] [[Gītā 15.18](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/15.18)]. To know Kṛṣṇa as `puruṣottama` is therefore to render `bhajana` to the independent Lord and not to collapse either `kṣara` or `akṣara` into him, for beyond `puruṣa` there is no higher principle and the finite self never becomes `svarūpataḥ` identical with Īśvara [[Gītā 15.19](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/15.19)] [[Kaṭha 1.3.11](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/Upaniṣad 1.3.11)] [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)].

In the indexed Gauḍīya locus, `puruṣottama` names the supreme personal object of `ahaitukī` and `avyavahitā` bhakti, so that the term is received first as a theological designation of Bhagavān within `bhajana` [[BRS 1.1.13](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.13)]. The word accordingly retains full personal force and marks the Lord toward whom uninterrupted devotion is directed [[BRS 1.1.13](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.13)].

The jīva is neither `atyantabhinna` nor `atyantābhinna` from Śrī Puruṣottama; it is his `aṃśa`, and that `aṃśatva` is understood as `śakti` rather than as a severed material part [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. The highest sense of `puruṣa` accordingly belongs to the Lord whose greatness exceeds the totality of beings, while the many selves subsist as dependent powers within his plenitude [[ChU 3.12.6](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/Upaniṣad 3.12.6)].
primary_loci: Chāndogya Upaniṣad 3.12.6 as cited in Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha

per_school.definition: Puruṣottama is the concrete Brahman to whose attainment brahmajñāna gives `svarūpa-yogyatā`, since the removal of avidyā readies the soul for its own proper condition in relation to the Lord [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)]. That attainment is consummated through `svīyatva` and `bhakti-bhāva`, not through abstraction from Bhagavān, so that `puruṣa` here retains a fully personal and grace-saturated sense [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

Yoga treats `puruṣa` as the conscious principle disclosed through discriminative practice, and in the indexed corpus Īśvara is defined as a `puruṣa-viśeṣa` untouched by `kleśa`, `karma`, `vipāka`, and `āśaya` [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)]. `Īśvara-praṇidhāna` is then interpreted not as mere recitation but as a specific cultivation of samādhi, so that the puruṣa-framework is preserved while contemplative orientation to the Lord is given a distinctive soteriological role [[YV 1.23](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23)].

Mādhyamaka does not admit `puruṣa` as an ultimately established self-substance; when the term appears, it does so within the conventional traffic of dependent designation [[VV 23](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23)]. The `māyāpuruṣa` simile shows that even agent, object, and negation can function without `svabhāva`, since the whole episode is intelligible precisely as illusion-like transaction [[VV 23](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23)].

Yogācāra uses analysis of experience to open entry into `pudgala-nairātmya`, since there is no single enduring seer or thinker standing behind the sixfold stream of consciousness [[Vvs 9](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/9 and Entry into *Pudgala-Nairātmya*)]. Accordingly, `puruṣa` appears only as represented content, like the men seen in dream or preta-experience, and not as an irreducible ontological witness [[Vvs 3](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Preta Replies)].

translator_note: 'Person', 'man', and 'spirit' all mislead if used without qualification. Modern English 'person' carries moral-legal and post-Lockean baggage; 'spirit' imports Christian and idealist associations. In Sāṃkhya, *puruṣa* is the witness distinct from the apparatus of action; in Vaiṣṇava and Vedāntic usage, it often names the supreme Person rather than the finite individual. The term should therefore be left untranslated unless the register is made explicit: witness-consciousness, cosmic Person, or supreme Person.
